data.title: Japan March Imports and Exports Edge Higher, As Trade Talks Underway
data.description:
Japan officials reported imports and exports edged higher in March, even as negotiators from Tokyo and Washington sat down to work out a possible new trade deal.

Japan's exports rose to 9.848 trillion yen in March, up 3.9% on year, reported the Ministry of Finance (MoF) on Thursday.

The nation's imports rose to 9.304 trillion yen, up 2% on year, resulting in a March trade surplus of 544.1 billion yen, the MoF added.

Japan and US officials met Wednesday at the White House to negotiate trade issues, in a session visited by President Donald Trump.

Japan exported 1.876 trillion yen worth of goods to the US in March, and imported 1.029 trillion worth, resulting in a March trade surplus with the US of 846.99 billion yen ($5.93 billion), according to MoF figures.

The Wednesday trade talks in Washington were preliminary and not expected to be conclusive.

Japan's Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba has indicated caution regarding the trade talks. "Of course, the negotiations will not be easy going forward, but President Trump has stated that he wants to give top priority to the talks with Japan," Ishiba said on Wednesday, reported Reuters.

Through March, new Trump Administration tariffs on Japan's exports to the US have not had much effect, said ING Think, an arm of the Dutch investment house.

"By destination, exports to the US still gained 3.1% despite tariffs on iron, steel and aluminum. Indeed, exports of iron and steel (down 16.6%) declined, but they only account for 1.3% of total exports. So far, the overall impact is muted. Major export items such as electrical machinery (up 6.1%) and transportation equipment (up 1.0%) gained, likely boosted by front-loading," said ING Think.

Japan exports to regional neighbors remained strong in March, although shipments to China sagged, added ING Think.

Shipments to Hong Kong rose 19.7% on year in March, while those to Taiwan rose 19.5% and those to South Korea rose by 11.5%, said ING Think.

However, exports to mainland China fell 4.8% on year in March, possibly reflecting certain US export controls, said ING Think.

data.title: SM Prime Buys Back More Shares
data.description:
SM Prime repurchased a further 149,000 shares on Tuesday at between 22.4 Philippine pesos and 22.5 pesos apiece, according to a Wednesday filing with the Philippine Stock Exchange.

The company has so far repurchased around 3 million shares under its current buyback program.

SM Prime is the property development arm of the Philippine conglomerate SM Investments .

data.title: Italy had positive GDP growth in first quarter, central bank says
data.description:
Italy's gross domestic product (GDP) is expected to have registered positive growth in the first quarter of the year, a Bank of Italy official said in a parliamentary hearing on Thursday, without giving a precise figure.

The euro zone's third-largest economy has been close to stagnation since mid-2024, with GDP edging up 0.1% in the fourth quarter of last year from the previous three months, after a flat reading in the third quarter.

"According to our estimates, GDP increased again in the first quarter of 2025," Andrea Brandolini, deputy head of the Bank of Italy's economy and statistics department, told lawmakers.

The national statistics office, Istat, is set to issue preliminary first quarter GDP data on April 30.

In its multi-year economic framework issued last week, the government cut its forecast for 2025 GDP growth to 0.6% from a projection of 1.2% made in September, and lowered its 2026 forecast to 0.8% from 1.1%.

A significant part of Italy's growth depends on billions of euros coming from the EU's COVID pandemic recovery funds, but Brandolini warned of delays and possible missed targets in their use.

"Delays are accumulating, especially in the execution of public works, with the risk that some goals and objectives will not be achieved by the deadline of the plan in August 2026", he said.

Brandolini noted that the government managed to spend only around 64 billion euros ($72.81 billion) through 2024, around one-third of available EU funds, and set itself very ambitious targets to recover lost ground.

The budget framework forecasts EU COVID funds expenditure of 40 billion euros in 2025, 80 billion euros in 2026 and 12 billion euros beyond 2026. "These are very high amounts if compared to the use of resources recorded so far," he said.

($1 = 0.8790 euros)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0616 ET - The surprise weakening of the U.S. dollar has created more room for global central banks to ease policy, says Fitch Ratings, which now expects deeper rate cuts from the ECB and emerging markets. As world growth slows, lower prices of commodities, including oil, will also spur a faster pace of monetary easing outside of the U.S., the ratings company says in a report. That will contrast with the Fed, which Fitch still expects to wait until 4Q to lower rates. The inflation picture is constraining the Fed's ability to loosen policy despite the deteriorating U.S. growth outlook, it says. Fitch expects the tariff escalation to drive U.S. inflation to 4.3% by the year's end, compared with its previous projection of 3.6%. (farah.elias@wsj.com)

0604 ET - Base metal prices are mixed, with LME three-month copper up 0.51% at $9,150.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.4% at $2,379.50 a ton. The U.S. copper industry has called on President Trump to place export restrictions on copper ore and scrap metal instead of imposing tariffs on imports, in order to boost domestic production. Recommendations include tax credits, streamlining permitting processes for new mines and tariffing imports of semi-fabricated products containing copper. Overall, given uncertainty around exactly how far copper prices can fall, investors should wait to buy copper until Trump reverses tariffs, fully redistributes tariff revenue, or the U.S. Federal Reserve or China start changing policy, MUFG analysts say in a note. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0548 ET - Bitcoin rises slightly as ongoing uncertainty over U.S. tariffs and cautious remarks from Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell about further interest rate cuts limit gains. President Trump said there was big progress in trade talks with Japan. However, investors remain skeptical given the lack of concrete results for now, especially between the U.S. and China, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Powell warned of higher inflation resulting from U.S. tariffs and stressed the need to keep longer-term inflation expectations anchored. Bitcoin rises 0.2% to $84,430, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0537 ET - Man Group reported what is likely one of its best flows performance for a first quarter but the recent tariff-triggered market weakness has taken a toll on the firm's assets under management, Panmure Liberum says in a research note. Client assets rose over the three months to March 31 on strong inflows but reversed course in the two first weeks of April. "The share price has already more than discounted that, leaving what is a world-leading company trading at a huge discount to its genuine value," analysts write. Shares fall around 5% to 151 pence, their lowest price since March 2021. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0434 ET - Futures linked to the tech-heavy Nasdaq-100 are on the rise early Thursday after Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. posted a jump in net profit and sales for the first quarter. TSMC's profit climbed 60% to 361.56 billion New Taiwan dollars, while revenue increased 42% from a year earlier. The E-mini Nasdaq 100 futures contract is up 1.2% on Thursday. The Nasdaq Composite index closed nearly 3.1% lower Wednesday after news of U.S. curbs on Nvidia's exports rattled investors in chip stocks. Nvidia stock closed 6.9% lower at $104.49 Wednesday. Shares are up 1% Thursday premarket, touching a high of $105.95. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0423 ET - U.S. long-dated Treasury yields could increase further if uncertainty surrounding U.S. trade policies lingers, analysts at UniCredit Investment Institute say in a note. Foreign private investors would likely sell long-dated Treasury holdings as a result, they say. "The rise in the market share of private foreign investors [in the Treasurys market] is a factor to consider because they do not usually have a buy-and-hold approach, likely leading to higher volatility." Private investor demand for Treasurys could resurface if the political and trade environment stabilizes, UniCredit says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0421 ET - European natural-gas prices rise in early trade, supported by a report that China would be open to trade talks with the U.S. under certain conditions. The benchmark Dutch TTF contract is up 1.2% to 35.74 euros a megawatt hour and on track for a weekly gain of more than 7%. Meanwhile, EU countries are seeking extra flexibility on the bloc's gas storage targets ahead of the winter season. "Our base case remains that European gas storage [levels] today are too low for inventories to reach the European Commission's target of 90% full by end October," analysts at MUFG say. "We view storage can still reach over 80% full, which will likely be sufficient to navigate winter 2025/26, given the ongoing ramp in global LNG supply." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0401 ET - Oil prices rise in early trade, with Brent crude above $66 a barrel after the U.S. imposed fresh sanctions aimed at curbing Iranian oil exports as part of the Trump administration's "maximum pressure" campaign on Tehran. The international oil benchmark trades 0.7% higher at $66.32 a barrel, while the U.S. oil gauge WTI is up 0.9% at $62.36 a barrel. Both contracts are on track for weekly gains, with Thursday marking the last settlement session of the week due to the Good Friday holiday. Meanwhile, some OPEC+ members submitted plans to make further output cuts to compensate for producing above quotas. Still, demand concerns linger, with Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell warning that consumers are likely to face higher prices as a result of U.S. tariffs and that the central bank could have less flexibility to quickly cushion the economy. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0256 ET - Spain's Santander has taken the crown from UBS as continental Europe's largest bank by market capitalization. The market turmoil triggered by the announcement of U.S. tariffs hit the Swiss lender's shares particularly hard given its higher levels of global diversification and exposure to markets compared with some of its peers. Santander's stock has gained 35% in value since Jan. 1 and was worth $103 billion as of Wednesday's close while UBS's market cap stood at $98 billion. French bank BNP Paribas and Italy's UniCredit follow, though London-listed HSBC remains the largest at $184 billion. The Stoxx 600 banks index--which UBS isn't part of--has gained almost 15% since the start of the year despite the tariff shock. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0240 ET - The dollar's recent selloff takes a pause as investors weigh the latest U.S. trade developments and remarks from Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell. President Trump said there was "big progress" in trade negotiations with Japan while Bloomberg reported that China was open to talks. Powell warned tariffs could lead to higher inflation and said the Fed's obligation is to keep longer-term inflation expectations "well anchored." The dollar still faces further falls as the "general sell U.S. vibe" persists, Pepperstone strategist Michael Brown says in a note. However, the currency has weakened considerably in a short space of time and could be "due something of a pause in the downside momentum." The DXY dollar index rises 0.3% to 99.6810. It hit a three-year low of 99.014 late last week. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0130 ET - The escalation in U.S.-China tariffs will hit the countries' trade flows dramatically, Fitch Ratings says in a special update to its quarterly global economic outlook. "With limited scope for import substitution or trade diversion in the near term, the adverse supply shock in the U.S. could be marked," analysts Brian Coulton and Alex Muscatelli write in the report. They raise their inflation forecast for the U.S. to more than 4%, implying a stagnation in real wages. The analysts note the Chinese economy's faster-than-expected expansion over the past year, but net trade has accounted for a third of GDP growth. "This will slow sharply as exporters struggle to redirect sales in the near term," they add. Fitch now expects below 4% growth for China and 1.2% growth for the U.S. in 2025. (farah.elias@wsj.com)

0053 ET - ResMed's bull at Goldman Sachs expects the breathing-tech manufacturer to use next week's 3Q result announcement to highlight its options to navigate U.S. tariff risks. Analyst Davin Thillainathan tells clients in a note that ResMed could use its Atlanta facility to supply the U.S. market. Its manufacturing is largely based in Australia and Singapore, both of which are subject to a 10% levy. Thillainathan estimates that tariffs will have a 4% negative impact on fiscal 2026 EPS. He thinks that ResMed's current share price discounts the company's growth potential and defensive attributes. MS keeps a buy rating and A$46.90 target price on the stock, which is up 0.85% at A$33.32. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

(giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0727 GMT - Aztech Global's 2025 outlook is poised to be challenging after its 1Q performance was dismal, CGS International's William Tng says in a research report. The miss to the brokerage's 1Q revenue and net profit forecasts for Aztech Global was due to reduced demand from the company's customers. Given the escalation in the U.S.-China trade conflict, the Singapore-listed company's revenue outlook could be weak over 2025-2027, prompting the brokerage to slash its revenue forecasts for Aztech Global by 41.6%-42.9% over that period. The brokerage cuts the stock's rating to reduce from hold and the target price to S$0.41 from S$0.67. Shares are 1.8% lower at S$0.555. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0630 GMT - Wipro's 1Q FY 2026 revenue growth guidance is likely weighed by tariff-induced macro uncertainty, Nomura analysts say. The Indian company guided for a sequential decline of -3.5% to -1.5%. Nomura notes that uncertainty is dampening demand in sectors such as consumer and manufacturing, pausing large transformation projects and affecting discretionary spending. A weak start in 1Q FY 2026 would pose a challenge to the technology services and consulting company's full-fiscal-year revenue growth outlook, says Nomura, lowering its FY 2026 revenue forecast to -3.9% from 0.0%. It also trims its target price for the stock to INR280.00 from INR300.00, with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 5.3% lower at INR234.45. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0559 GMT - Energy prices will continue to fall on year throughout 2025, keeping eurozone price inflation lower, Pantheon Macroeconomics' Claus Vistesen and Melanie Debono write in a note to clients. Global oil and gas prices have fallen in recent weeks amid lower demand expectations and fears of an economic slowdown. With that trend set to continue, eurozone energy prices will be on average 1% lower on year over 2025, according to Pantheon's calculations, which reverse previous estimates for moderate inflation in energy over the year. That will take a couple of decimal points off headline inflation, Vistesen and Debono say, with knock-on implications for monetary policy in the currency area. "The European Central Bank will be drawing the same conclusion in its June [inflation] forecasts, unless oil prices rebound strongly," the economists say. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0544 GMT - Siemens Energy's second-quarter result will likely drive a double-digit upgrade to consensus, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. The German company raised its fiscal-year guidance after adjusted profit beat consensus expectations by 57%. "All in, this is a very strong result and therefore the timing of guidance upgrade amid tariff uncertainty isn't surprising," the analysts say. Consensus expectations could rise between 20% and 40% on the back of the improved outlook, they say. The scale of the upgrade and the implied margins for the second-half of the year suggest Siemens Energy isn't expecting any material impact from U.S. tariffs. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Central Bank Easing Could Pick Up; Fed Likely to Wait Until 4QMarket Talk
data.description:
1016 GMT - The surprise weakening of the U.S. dollar has created more room for global central banks to ease policy, says Fitch Ratings, which now expects deeper rate cuts from the ECB and emerging markets. As world growth slows, lower prices of commodities, including oil, will also spur a faster pace of monetary easing outside of the U.S., the ratings company says in a report. That will contrast with the Fed, which Fitch still expects to wait until 4Q to lower rates. The inflation picture is constraining the Fed's ability to loosen policy despite the deteriorating U.S. growth outlook, it says. Fitch expects the tariff escalation to drive U.S. inflation to 4.3% by the year's end, compared with its previous projection of 3.6%. (farah.elias@wsj.com)

1015 GMT - The recent jump in U.K. long-dated gilt yields could be short-lived as the move was mainly driven by increases in U.S. long-dated Treasury yields yet with "less underlying justification," Citi's Jamie Searle says in a note. Thirty-year gilt yields reached a multi-year high of 5.663% on April 9. They have since retreated and last trade at 5.362%, albeit around 11 basis points higher since the start of April, Tradeweb data show. U.K. curve steepening--where the difference between short- and long-dated yields increases--"may have overshot somewhat and recent distortions may prove temporary," Searle says. There is less justification for capital flight from the U.K. as there is from the U.S., he says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0955 GMT - Beijing could unveil more favorable policies to boost domestic consumption to offset slower exports, Citi analysts say in a research note. Such policies could include extension of trade-in programs and local consumption and traveling vouchers, they say. As internet giants step up investment commitments to support Beijing's initiatives, "we are concerned that this could translate to excess domestic supply and profit/margins pressure especially if revs growth moderates amid softened economic outlook," Citi says. Given delisting risks for Chinese ADRs, Citi expects U.S.-listed Chinese companies to seek Hong Kong dual listings and speed up primary listing conversion for those already dual-listed. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0952 GMT - The fair value of 10-year German Bund yields is around 2.75%, above current levels, says BlueBay CIO Mark Dowding in a note. Bunds with yields below 2.5% are expensive, he says. BlueBay AM thinks money markets are pricing in too many interest-rate cuts by the European Central Bank, given prospective fiscal easing. The 10-year Bund yield rises 3 basis points to 2.536%, according to Tradeweb. Money markets price in the ECB's terminal deposit rate at around 1.70%, implying more than three rate cuts from the current 2.5% level, according to LSEG. The ECB announces a decision at 1215 GMT and is widely expected to cut interest rates. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0951 GMT - China still has ample fiscal room and various stimulus tools at its disposal to safeguard its economy, Barclays analysts say in a research note. Beijing can offset the tariff pain if it chooses to do so, and will put a floor under growth, the analysts say. "The Chinese economy and its public have become more accustomed to (external) shocks and more resilient to endure some prolonged pain if necessary." The upside surprise in China's 1Q GDP growth was driven by better-than-expected consumption, exports and infrastructure and manufacturing investment, they say. The analysts expect additional fiscal support from Beijing by summer, depending on economic developments. Growth momentum will likely moderate in 2Q and 3Q after a strong rebound in 4Q last year and some front-loading of policy stimulus in 1Q, they add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0948 GMT - Bitcoin rises slightly as ongoing uncertainty over U.S. tariffs and cautious remarks from Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell about further interest rate cuts limit gains. President Trump said there was big progress in trade talks with Japan. However, investors remain skeptical given the lack of concrete results for now, especially between the U.S. and China, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Powell warned of higher inflation resulting from U.S. tariffs and stressed the need to keep longer-term inflation expectations anchored. Bitcoin rises 0.2% to $84,430, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0939 GMT - Concerns about weaker U.S. growth resulting from tariffs are largely behind sterling's recent gains against the dollar, Convera strategist George Vessey says in a note. However, this week's softer-than-expected U.K. inflation and labor market data have also weighed on sterling, he says. "This has cemented market expectations of a Bank of England rate cut in May, with two more cuts fully priced in by year-end." Against the euro, however, sterling has recently weakened as the euro benefits from higher liquidity and an ongoing repatriation of assets into the eurozone. Sterling trades flat at $1.3244 after hitting a six-month high of $1.3293 Wednesday, according to FactSet. The euro falls 0.2% to 0.8590 pounds after reaching a 15-month high of 0.8738 on Friday.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0930 GMT - The European Central Bank's policy decision later could have little impact on the euro as it remains driven by U.S. tariff news, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. The ECB is widely expected to cut rates by 25 basis points in a decision at 1215 GMT. The foreign exchange market isn't currently looking much at short-term rate differentials, he says. "If it did, the euro should be trading well below $1.10." The highly liquid euro remains in a prime position to benefit from a rotation away from the dollar amid concerns about weaker U.S. growth due to tariffs, he says. The euro falls 0.2% to $1.1379 and ING expects it to reach $1.15 in the near-term.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0833 GMT - The cost of insuring euro-denominated against default using credit default swaps declines as investors await the European Central Bank interest rate decision at 1215 GMT where a 25 basis-point cut is widely expected. "The ECB will likely deliver a 25bp rate cut to respond to intensifying downside risks to economic activity," analysts at the UniCredit Investment Institute say in a note. The iTraxx Europe Crossover index which tracks euro junk bond credit default swaps falls 3bp to 364bp, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. The iTraxx Europe Main index which tracks euro investment-grade CDS declines 1bp to 71bp. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0823 GMT - U.S. long-dated Treasury yields could increase further if uncertainty surrounding U.S. trade policies lingers, analysts at UniCredit Investment Institute say in a note. Foreign private investors would likely sell long-dated Treasury holdings as a result, they say. "The rise in the market share of private foreign investors [in the Treasurys market] is a factor to consider because they do not usually have a buy-and-hold approach, likely leading to higher volatility." Private investor demand for Treasurys could resurface if the political and trade environment stabilizes, UniCredit says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0815 GMT - The Bank of Korea's next rate cut could come as early as next month, given its incrementally more dovish forward guidance for rate moves, Goldman Sachs economist Goohoon Kwon writes in a note. The BOK is forecast to cut the base rate by 25 basis points each in May and August, with all six board members--excluding its governor--now being open to more rate cuts over the next three months, up from two members previously, Kwon says. The pace of BOK policy easing could be faster and deeper given the substantial downside risks to growth due mainly to U.S. tariff risks, he adds.(kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0810 GMT - The Turkish lira could fall if Turkey's central bank sounds complacent about the risks of higher inflation in a decision at 1200 GMT, Commerzbank's Tatha Ghose says in a note. The central bank faces a "challenging backdrop," he says. The moderation in March inflation was misleading as the month-on-month rate of increase in core inflation was elevated. Market inflation expectations in the latest central bank survey also rose. "And, all this was even before the inflation pass-through of last month's lira depreciation has been reflected in prices." Despite aggressive interventions to defend the lira, volatility has persisted since the market blow-up when Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan's rival Ekrem Imamoglu was arrested. The dollar trades flat at 38.1561 lira. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: FundedNext Launches "Futures" Prop Firm with Industry-First Rewards - Now Accepting U.S. Traders in Their Futures Platform
data.description:
• FundedNextIs Back in the U.S.-For Futures Only - U.S. traders can now join the most rewarding Futures prop firm with instant access and zero restrictions.

  • Get Paid in 24 Hours-Or Get $1,000 Extra - Enjoy lightning-fast Performance Rewards with our 24-hour performance reward guarantee, the first of its kind in Futures prop trading.

AFZ, AJMAN, UAE / ACCESS Newswire / April 17, 2025 / FundedNext, one of the leading prop trading firms known for its reliability over the years & unique ‘trader-first' rewards approach, has officially entered the Futures space with the launch of FundedNext Futures.

This is available exclusively on the FundedNext Futures platform. While CFD products remain unavailable for U.S. clients, Futures traders in the U.S. can now access one of the most rewarding prop models in the world.

FundedNext began with the ambitious goal of impacting 50 million traders' lives worldwide. In an industry where firms often rise and fall quickly, FundedNext has maintained a steady presence-growing past all barriers and earning a reputation for reliability, innovation, and a trader-first approach. Furthermore, The Futures prop trading industry has rapidly grown over the past few years, with more traders entering the space than ever before.

Shortly after their 3-year anniversary, FundedNext announced its entry into the Futures industry-marking a step toward its ultimate goal of becoming the complete prop trading ecosystem.

"When we started FundedNext, it was with the belief that prop trading could be done differently-with fairness, speed, and transparency. Extending that same vision into Futures was a natural evolution. What we're building is not just a product, but a step toward a complete prop trading ecosystem, for every trader, every market," said Syed Abdullah Jayed, CEO of FundedNext.

Building on this idea, "At FundedNext, our move into futures trading is driven by conversations with real futures traders. They've shared challenges around clarity in rules, timely payouts, and consistent trading conditions. We're here to address those pain points directly, creating an environment built around transparency, stability, and genuine career progression," said Syed Abdullah Galib, Chief Strategy Officer of FundedNext.

FundedNext Enters Futures Prop Trading with Transparent, Trader-Centric Model

FundedNext has officially launched its Futures prop trading division, introducing a new approach to an industry many traders feel has grown stagnant. With a focus on transparency and trader empowerment, the firm aims to shift the narrative from restrictive models to one that recognizes and rewards genuine performance.

Key Highlights of the FundedNext Futures Model

  • Performance Rewards From the Challenge Phase

    In what appears to be a first for the Futures prop trading industry, traders are eligible to receive a 15% performance reward from the initial Challenge Phase-a reward structure famously first introduced by FundedNext in their CFD firm challenges.

  • Performance Reward Within 24 Hours, with an Unusual Guarantee

    FundedNext Futures commits to processing rewards within 24 hours of reward request. If this timeline isn't met, traders receive an additional $1,000-another FundedNext exclusive policy in the prop trading industry that underscores the firm's emphasis on timely rewards.

  • Full Retention of Performance Rewards

    Once funded, traders retain 100% of their performance rewards. There are no reward splits or deductions applied, offering a level of earning transparency that many in the trading community have advocated for.

  • No Ongoing Platform Fees

    Access to trading accounts is provided via a one-time payment model. There are no monthly charges, activation fees, or hidden costs-an effort to simplify the financial commitment for traders.

  • Scaling and Performance Reward Flexibility

    The platform allows traders to scale up to 10 mini contracts and withdraw performance rewards every five days. Additionally, there are no restrictions around trading during news events-providing more operational freedom.

FundedNext Futures Launches with Global Access, Now Open to U.S. Traders

With the launch of its new Futures platform, FundedNext expands its reach to include traders from over 170 countries-now including the United States. The platform supports a wide range of payment & withdrawal methods, including but not limited to local & international payment options, bank transfers, e-wallets, and cryptocurrencies, aiming to offer broad access and faster cross-border payments.

Responding to Calls for Greater Transparency

In an industry where traders have increasingly voiced concerns over lack of control and vague rules, FundedNext Futures enters with a focus on transparency and performance-based progression. Instead of focusing on time limits or trading volume, the platform is structured to reward traders based on their actual performance.

A Shift in Prop Trading Models?

While many prop trading firms continue to operate under traditional fee and performance reward models, FundedNext Futures introduces an alternative framework. Key elements include performance-based rewards from the Challenge Phase Reward, flexible scaling, and shorter withdrawal cycles-features that may appeal to traders seeking more control and fewer delays.

Now Live

The FundedNext Futures website is currently live and open for registration. More information can be found at: https://www.fundednext.com/Futures

About FundedNext

FundedNext is a globally recognized financial technology company and modern proprietary trading firm specializing in simulated funding programs. FundedNext offers traders simulated trading accounts designed to closely replicate real market conditions, enabling clients from over 170 countries to effectively develop and refine their trading skills. FundedNext further supports trader development through expert educational resources, a thriving trading community, strategic networking opportunities, and advanced analytical tools.

Successful traders benefit from generous reward-sharing structures, receiving up to 100% performance rewards, including an industry-first 15% rewards from Challenge Phase, rewarding their expertise from the very beginning. FundedNext is also expanding its ecosystem by strategically launching its own CFD brokerage division, further enhancing the overall trading experience for its global client base.

data.title: Hermes to Raise Prices in the U.S. to Mitigate Tariff Hit2nd Update
data.description:
By Andrea Figueras

Birkin bag maker Hermes plans to lift prices in the U.S. to mitigate the impact of tariffs, as the luxury sector braces for a global trade war unfolding against a background of waning demand.

The French luxury giant said it hasn't experienced any impact from President Trump's tariffs so far, but added that it made a slow start to the year in the U.S. and recorded no sales growth in China. Overall, the group's first-quarter sales grew 7.2% when adjusted for currency movements, a slowdown from the 18% increase it logged for the previous quarter.

Hermes is calculating the price hikes in order to offset the impact that duties could have on growth, Eric du Halgouet, the company's executive vice-president of finance, said Thursday during a call with analysts. The price increases will apply only to the U.S. since they are aimed at mitigating tariffs in the American market, he said.

This shows how even companies that cater to the ultrarich are positioning themselves for an era of rising trade tensions. Trump's sweeping tariff plans have prompted businesses across sectors to brace for turbulence--some are trying to pass on the extra costs to customers, others are seeking to cut costs to absorb the hit, and some have withdrawn guidance due to uncertainty.

"We haven't seen any impact, but we remain very conservative," du Halgouet said.

Analysts see Hermes as better placed than most to navigate tariffs due to its pricing power. Hermes has shown resilience in a tough climate for luxury brands, especially in the crucial Chinese market, as it relies on an affluent clientele that continued to spend. By contrast, luxury labels that target a less rich shoppers faced greater difficulties.

For the first quarter, Hermes reported revenue of 4.13 billion euros ($4.71 billion), up from 3.805 billion euros a year before. The result compares with analysts' forecasts of 4.14 billion euros, according to a Visible Alpha poll.

Despite the deceleration, Hermes posted growth in all regions, including the Americas. Low stock levels contributed to a slow start to the year for the group in the U.S., du Halgouet said. However, the company's performance was positive in March and trends haven't changed as of early April, he added.

A pickup in U.S. demand helped several luxury companies, including Hermes, to finish 2024 on a strong note, though analysts had expected a weaker start to 2025 amid uncertainty about the global economy and Trump's tariffs.

Earlier this week, luxury giant LVMH reported weaker-than-expected sales for the quarter, pointing to a slow start to the year for a company that is considered a bellwether for the sector. Shares in LVMH--the conglomerate behind brands like Louis Vuitton, Dior and Tiffany--tumbled after the results, allowing Hermes to leapfrog it to become the world's most valuable luxury company.

Shares in Hermes traded 1.2% lower in midday trading in Europe.

Looking ahead, the company said it continues to target revenue growth at constant currency in the medium term despite the economic, geopolitical and monetary uncertainties.

Write to Andrea Figueras at andrea.figueras@wsj.com

On 17 April 2025, Roland Carter and Julian Fagge, both Executive Directors of Smiths Group plc, were granted share awards under the rules of the 2024 Smiths Group plc Long Term Incentive Plan ('Awards'), and in accordance with the Company's Directors' Remuneration Policy. The Awards were equivalent to 50% of salary for each Director and were granted as conditional share awards over ordinary shares in the Company.

To align Executive Director and shareholder interests and reflecting the strategic focus on unlocking shareholder value, the performance conditions for the Awards will be a relative TSR metric measured over three financial years beginning FY25. The metric will measure the Company's share price and dividend performance against a set peer group, being the FTSE100 (excluding Financial Services and Investment Trust companies). Threshold performance will be set at 25% vesting at the 60th percentile level, increasing on a straight-line basis to maximum vesting at the 90th percentile.

data.title: Hooker Furnishings Corporation Reports Q4 and Full-Year Fiscal 2025 Results
data.description:
Hooker Furnishings Corporation, a global leader in the design, production, and marketing of home furnishings, has released its operating results for the fiscal 2025 fourth quarter and full-year ended February 2, 2025. The fiscal 2025 fourth quarter and full year comprised 14 weeks and 53 weeks, respectively, compared to 13 weeks and 52 weeks in the previous year.

Financial Highlights

For the fiscal 2025 fourth quarter, the company reported net sales of $104.5 million, an 8% increase from $96.8 million in the prior year quarter. However, the company experienced a consolidated operating loss of $2.7 million, compared to an operating income of $340,000 in the prior year quarter. The consolidated net loss for the quarter was $2.3 million, or ($0.22) per diluted share, compared to a net income of $593,000, or $0.06 per diluted share, in the prior year quarter.

For the full year of fiscal 2025, consolidated net sales were $397.5 million, reflecting an 8.3% decrease from the previous fiscal year. The company reported a consolidated operating loss of $18.1 million, compared to an operating income of $12.4 million in the prior year. The consolidated net loss for the year was $12.5 million, or ($1.19) per diluted share, compared to a net income of $9.9 million, or $0.91 per diluted share, in the prior year.

Business and Operational Highlights

Hooker Branded's fourth quarter net sales rose by 10.0%, driven by a 14% increase in unit volume. Home Meridian's fourth quarter net sales increased by 21.7%, driven by strong hospitality sales. However, Domestic Upholstery's fourth quarter net sales decreased by 7.0% year-over-year due to soft demand across various divisions.

Despite the losses, fiscal 2025 milestones included the Margaritaville licensing agreement, the launch of Hooker Branded’s new merchandising strategy, Sunset West’s bi-coastal expansion, key inventory investments, and share gains amid a tough market.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

The company announced additional planned cost savings, expecting fiscal 2026 cost savings of at least $0.8 to $1.0 million from the Savannah warehouse exit. The company also expects annualized cost savings from this exit of between $4.0 to $5.7 million beginning in fiscal 2027. Combined with other efforts, the company expects to realize a portion of the expected $18 to $20 million in annual operating expense savings by mid-year fiscal 2026.

Management's Perspective

CEO Jeremy R. Hoff commented, “Excluding these charges, our financial performance improved sequentially each quarter throughout the year. Even considering the extra week, Hooker Branded and Home Meridian sales increased.” Hoff also highlighted the company's market share growth and the strategic initiatives aimed at improving operating income and cash flow.

Future Outlook

The company is currently evaluating strategies to mitigate the current economic environment, including the impact of tariffs and a weak housing market. Despite these challenges, the company remains focused on gaining market share and maximizing revenues through merchandising efforts, speed-to-market initiatives, and maintaining an in-stock position on top-selling products.
